coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots disturbed the energy homeostasis by influencing lipid metabolism of macrophages
coredata.dc:creator: Shao, Yan
Wang, Xiaoyang
Wang, Liting
Huang, Youying
Wei, Quanfang
Sun, Wei
Lai, Xiaodan
Yang, Fan
Li, Fei
Huang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: To investigate the potential factors of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the assessment impact on the innate immune system is one of the most important. As the innate immune cell, macrophages possess phagocytosis activity and affect immunomodulation. Higher oxygen consumption rates (OCR) are used to gain insight into GQDs’ effects on macrophages. Metabolomics profiling also revealed that GQDs exposure provoked an increase in phosphoglycerides, sphingolipids, and oxidized lipids in macrophages. The molecular pathways disrupted by GQDs were associated with lipid and energy metabolisms. Metabolite flux analysis was used to evaluate changes in the lipid metabolism of macrophages exposed to 100 µg mL−1 GQDs for 24 and 48 h. A combination of 13C-flux analysis and metabolomics revealed the regulation of lipid biosynthesis influenced the balance of energy metabolism. Integrated proteomics and metabolomics analyses showed that nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide and coenzyme Q10 were significantly increased under GQDs treatment, alongside upregulated protein activity (e.g., Cox5b and Cd36). The experimental evidences were expected to be provided in this study to reveal the potential harmful effect from exposure to GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Metabolomics
13C-metabolic flux analysis
Energy homeostasis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exposure to GQDs affected specific metabolism pathways
Metabolic flux characterization of macrophages exposed GQDs
Correlation analysis between proteomics and metabolomics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Seahorse cell mito stress test for OCR of RAW 264.7 macrophages with and without 100 µg mL−1 GQD treatment for 48 h, and (b) Basal and maximal OCR graphs. Data are mean ± SD (n = 5 independent experiments).

Metabolomic analysis. (a) PCA of metabolites identified by LC-MS/MS, (b) OPLS-DA score plots, with metabolites differentially grouped by control (green) and test (red) RAW 264.7 macrophage cells, (c) Volcano plots showing the expression of differential metabolites between control and test groups, (d) HCA of the differential metabolites, with red and green segments representing relatively high and low metabolite abundance, respectively, and (e) KEGG pathway analysis of differential metabolites between control and GQD-treated RAW 264.7 macrophages.

Metabolic flux analysis of RAW 264.7 macrophages cultured with GQDs for 24 and 48 h. (a) Heatmap of altered lipid metabolites, and (b) Qualitative changes in metabolic pathway contributions: 13C tracer analysis identifies changes in fatty acids.
Pathway analysis of the metabolome and proteome.

(a) Corheatmap analysis of metabolites and proteins: CCA of KEGG pathways for metabolites (violet) and proteins (blue), and fold changes of upregulated (red) and downregulated (blue) metabolites and proteins, (b) CCA of AMPK signaling pathway, (c) CCA of OXPHOS, and (d) CCA of glycerolipid metabolism.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots disturbed the energy homeostasis by influencing lipid metabolism of macrophages

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Clinical Pharmacy, Cancer Center, Department of Pharmacy, Zhejiang Provincial People's Hospital (Affiliated People's Hospital, Hangzhou Medical College), Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Rocket Force Medicine, State Key Laboratory of Trauma, Burns and Combined Injury, Army Medical University, Chongqing 400038, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Rocket Force Medicine, State Key Laboratory of Trauma, Burns and Combined Injury, Army Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Rocket Force Medicine, State Key Laboratory of Trauma, Burns and Combined Injury, Army Medical University, Chongqing, 400038, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, No. 958 Hospital of PLA, Southwest Hospital, Army Military Medical University, Chongqing 400020, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacy, No. 958 Hospital of PLA, Southwest Hospital, Army Military Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, No.958 Hospital of PLA, Southwest Hospital, Army Military Medical University, Chongqing, 400020, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate the potential factors of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), the assessment impact on the innate immune system is one of the most important. As the innate immune cell, macrophages possess phagocytosis activity and affect immunomodulation. Higher oxygen consumption rates (OCR) are used to gain insight into GQDs’ effects on macrophages. Metabolomics profiling also revealed that GQDs exposure provoked an increase in phosphoglycerides, sphingolipids, and oxidized lipids in macrophages. The molecular pathways disrupted by GQDs were associated with lipid and energy metabolisms. Metabolite flux analysis was used to evaluate changes in the lipid metabolism of macrophages exposed to 100 µg mL−1 GQDs for 24 and 48 h. A combination of 13C-flux analysis and metabolomics revealed the regulation of lipid biosynthesis influenced the balance of energy metabolism. Integrated proteomics and metabolomics analyses showed that nicotinic acid adenine dinucleotide and coenzyme Q10 were significantly increased under GQDs treatment, alongside upregulated protein activity (e.g., Cox5b and Cd36). The experimental evidences were expected to be provided in this study to reveal the potential harmful effect from exposure to GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotheranostics have promising opportunities in medical fields, including precise diagnosis and effective therapy (Liu et al., 2020; Zarepour et al., 2019). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used as favorable candidates for biomedical applications, owing to their ultra-small size, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and excellent photostability, etc (Xuan et al., 2018; Xue et al., 2019). Because of surface passivation, GQDs show high fluorescence and properties with a photoluminescence quantum efficiency, offering unparalleled potential in bioimaging for accurate diagnosis. (Li et al., 2017; Younis et al., 2020). Due to their important potential applications, GQDs toxicology requires a complete assessment before clinical use to ensure biological safety. Recent studies have shown that GQD has induced a wide range of adverse effects such as cytotoxicity, inflammation and genotoxicity (de Luna et al., 2022; Sun et al., 2022). At the same time, toxicological studies have shown that graphene increases inflammatory cytokine production, indicating changes in immune system activity (Kinaret et al., 2020; Mukherjee et al., 2017; Orecchioni et al., 2016).

Macrophages play an important part in the innate immune response. They are capable of performing phagocytosis, which is a way to intracellularly confine potentially dangerous materials (Papini et al., 2020; Yazdimamaghani et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019). Affected cells were realized as a global network including genes, transcripts, proteins, and metabolites, and the biochemical interactions among these molecules (Ohno et al., 2022). Metabolites including glucose, lipids, and amino acids mainly regard as downstream products, directly modulate protein activity, and biological processes for further mechanistic studies (Guijas et al., 2018). Quantitative metabolic flux had greatly advanced a more particular knowledge of the quantitative flow of carbon and changes in pathways (Rinschen et al., 2019), understanding mechanisms of toxicity. Owing to its graphene nature, macrophages’ uptake of the nanoparticles relates to the induction of intracellular active modulates (Ayoubi et al. ,2017; Ju et al., 2019; Sies et al., 2017). Therefore, a thorough investigation is needed to understand the underlying molecular mechanisms of macrophages respond to GQDs.

In the present study, RAW264.7 macrophage cells were exposed to GQDs concentrations of 100 µg mL−1, with an exposure time of 48 h. Mitochondrial respiration was indicative of mitochondrial function showing GQDs were effective in increasing oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) and ATP production. The metabolomics data were analyzed to investigate the abnormal changes of signaling pathways associated with glycerophospholipid and sphingolipid metabolism. Stable isotope labeling 13C was used to monitor the profiles of lipid molecular species under time-dependent GQDs, encompassing metabolic fluxes of lipid molecular species in the lipid biosynthetic and decomposition pathways. Then, we combined untargeted metabolomics with quantitative proteomics to identify which pathways in macrophages were influenced by GQDs. The omics-analysis demonstrated the potential molecular mechanisms effect of GQDs and revealed previously unrecognized relationships of lipid metabolism in energy homeostasis impact of macrophages.

GQDs were purchased by First Graphene Co, Ltd, Chongqing, China. Physicochemical properties of GQDs were provided by our previous results (Shao et al., 2021). Gradient grades of methanol, acetonitrile, methyl tertiary butyl ether (MTBE), and isopropanol were purchased from Merck Company (Germany), acetic acid and standards and other chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The standards 12:0 Lyso PC and Cer (d 18:1/4:0) were purchased from Avanti. D-GLUCOSE (U-13C6, 99%) was purchased from Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Inc.

Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8; Dojindo Laboratories, Kyushu, Japan) was used to assess the cell viability according to the manufacturer’s instructions. Briefly, RAW264.7 macrophages were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated overnight. The GQDs were dispersed in HBSS and were cocultured with the cells. The GQDs were added at the indicated concentrations (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 µg mL−1) and the cells were further incubated for 48 h. The absorbance values (OD450) were measured using a Versa Max Microplate Reader (BioTek, Winooski, Vermont).

RAW264.7 macrophage cells were seeded at a density of 2000 cells/well in a XF96 Seahorse plate. Cells were cultured in normal-glucose DMEM (GIBCO) with 10% FBS (GIBCO) overnight. Here, we measured energy metabolism in macrophages by using the protocol and algorithm program in Seahorse XF96 Extracellular Flux analyzer (Agilent). This machine measured glycolysis by analyzing the extracellular acidification rate (ECAR) and mitochondrial OXPHOS on the basis of the oxygen consumption rate (OCR), through real-time and live cell analysis. Oligomycin (1.5 μM), FCCP (1 μM), and rotenone (0.5 μM) with antimycin A (0.5 μM) (R + A) were added acutely to the wells, and respiratory parameters calculated. Before the analysis, the medium was incubated at 37 °C in a non-CO2 incubator for 45 min. Five technical replicates were analyzed for each biological replicate. Basal OCR was calculated as [OCR initial – OCRR+A]. ATP production: the last rate measurement before oligomycin injection minus the minimum rate measurement after oligomycin injection. Maximum respiration rate was computed as [OCR FCCP – OCRR+A].
1 × 106 RAW264.7 macrophage cells were implanted into 10 cm culture dish and incubated with the medium containing 100 µg mL−1 GQDs for 48 h (n = 5).

Sample was added 1 mL pre-cooled extractant (80% methanol aqueous solution), and whirl for 2 min. Freeze the mixture for 3 min in liquid nitrogen after remove ice for 5 min, it will be whirled for 2 min, repeated 3 times. Centrifuge the mixture again with 12,000 g/min at 4 ℃ for 10 min. Finally take 200 μL of supernatant into the inner liner of the corresponding injection bottle for on-board analysis.

The analytical conditions were as follows, UPLC: column, Waters ACQUITY UPLC HSS T3 C18 (1.8 µm, 2.1 mm * 100 mm); column temperature, 40 ℃; flow rate, 0.4 mL/min; injection volume, 2 μL; solvent system, water (0.1% formic acid): acetonitrile (0.1% formic acid); gradient program, (V/V) 95:5 at 0 min, 10:90 at 11.0 min,10:90 at 12.0 min, 95:5 at 12.1 min, 95:5 at 14.0 min

The ESI source operation parameters were as follows: source temperature 500 ℃; ion spray voltage (IS) 5500 V (positive), − 4500 V (negative); ion source gas I (GSI), gas II (GSII), curtain gas (CUR) were set at 55, 60, and 25.0 psi, respectively; the collision gas (CAD) was high.

Samples placed in liquid nitrogen for 2 min, then thawed on ice for 5 min and vortex blending, repeated 3 times, then centrifuged it with 5000 g at 4 ℃ for 1 min. Homogenize it with 1 mL mixture (include methanol, MTBE and internal standard mixture) (Dong et al., 2022; Matyash et al., 2008). Whirl the mixture for 15 min. Then add 200 μL of water and whirl the mixture for 1 min, and centrifuge it with 12,000 g at 4 ℃ for 10 min. Extract 500 μL supernatant and concentrate it. Dissolve powder with 200 μL mobile phase B. Finally take the dissolving solution into the sample bottle for LC-MS/MS analysis.

The analytical conditions were as follows, UPLC: column, Thermo Accucore™ C30 (2.6 µm, 2.1 mm * 100 mm i.d.); column temperature, 45 ℃; flow rate, 0.35 mL/min; injection volume: 2 μL; solvent system, A: acetonitrile/water (60/40, V/V, 0.1% formic acid, 10 mmol L−1 ammonium formate), B: acetonitrile/isopropanol (10/90, V/V, 0.1% formic acid, 10 mmol L−1 ammonium formate); gradient program, A/B (V/V) 80:20, at 0 min, 70:30, at 2.0 min, 40:60, at 4 min, 15:85 at 9 min, 10:90 at 14 min, 5:95 at 15.5 min, 5:95 at 17.3 min, 80:20 at 17.3 min, 80:20 at 20 min

The ESI source operation parameters were as follows: ion source, turbo spray; source temperature 500 ℃; ion spray voltage (IS) 5500 V(positive), − 4500 V(negative); ion source gas 1 (GS1), gas 2 (GS2), curtain gas (CUR) were set at 45, 55, and 35 psi, respectively; the collision gas (CAD) was medium.

Sample extracts were analyzed using an LC-ESI-MS/MS system (ExionLC AD UPLC, https://sciex.com.cn/, QTRAP MS System, https://sciex.com/). LIT and triple quadrupole (QQQ) scans were acquired on a triple quadrupole-linear ion trap mass spectrometer QTRAP LC-MS/MS system equipped with an ESI turbo ion-spray interface, operating in positive and negative ion modes and controlled by Analyst 1.6.3 software (Sciex). Principal component analysis (PCA) was performed using the statistics function prcomp in R (www.r-project.org). Orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis (OPLS-DA) scores plots and permutation plots were generated using R package MetaboAnalystR. Hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) of samples and metabolites was carried out using the R package ComplexHeatmap. Identified metabolites were annotated using the KEGG Compound database (http://www.kegg.jp/kegg/compound/) and mapped using the KEGG Pathway database (http://www.kegg.jp/kegg/pathway.html). Cytoscape 3.8 was used to visualize protein-metabolite interactions. Schematic necroptosis signaling pathways were created using BioRender.com.

Cold extraction buffer (500 μL, methanol:acetonitrile:water, 2:2:1, v/v/v) was added to each sample, sonicated for 2 min, and centrifuged at 14,000 g for 5 min at 4 °C. Supernatants were thoroughly lyophilized (FreeZone 6 Liter, Labconco, USA) and reconstituted in 50 μL of methanol:water (1:1, v/v) immediately before analysis. Isotopologue distribution was determined using a Thermo Q Exactive Plus hybrid quadrupole–orbitrap mass spectrometer coupled to a Thermo Vanquish UPLC system. Data processing and ion annotation based on accurate mass were performed using TraceFinder 5.0 (Thermo Fisher) and Xcalibur 4.0 (Thermo Fisher).

As the primary source of cellular energy, mitochondrial respiration, alongside cellular ATP levels (Poderoso et al., 2019), were the important indicators of the state of a cell. First, CCK8 assays were conducted to assess the cellular viability of incubating RAW264.7 macrophages with GQDs at different concentrations (0, 10, 25, 50, 100 and 200 µg mL−1) for 48 h. There was no difference in cell activity between the control group and less than 100 µg mL−1 groups. As GQDs concentration increased to 100 and 200 µg mL−1, the cell viability decreased to 85.29% and 73.55% (Fig. S1). Based on the cell viability assay and electron microscopy had been performed, no matter macrophages’ viability or damage of mitochondria significant differences at a GQD concentration of 100 µg mL−1 48 h (Shao et al., 2021). The Cell Mito Stress Test was used to measure oxygen consumption rate (OCR) and estimate key parameters of mitochondrial respiration in real-time in the presence of 100 µg mL−1 GQDs for 48 h ( Fig. 1a). Basal respiration, mitochondrial-dependent ATP generation, and maximal respiration capacity were increased compared to the control (Fig. 1b), demonstrating the impact of GQDs on macrophage mitochondrial respiration and redox function. Almost all energy production in mammalian cells originates from mitochondrial ATP synthesis (Fernandez-Vizarra and Zeviani, 2021). Fatty acids are one of the main sources of energy and are catabolized to produce ATP via β-oxidation (Ibrahim et al., 2020). Metabolomics can be used to profile the global metabolic changes of macrophages exposed to GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exposure to GQDs affected specific metabolism pathways
ce:section.ce:para: The untargeted LC-MS/MS metabolomics data were interrogated by applying PCA to the control (C) and GQD-treated groups (T). The PCA score plot showed total component 55.72%, first principal component 38.56%, and second principal component 17.16%, and clear separation of control and treated groups ( Fig. 2a) that indicated a perturbation of the cell metabolome after 48 h exposure to GQDs. The OPLS-DA score plot (Fig. 2b) also showed clear separation of the groups on the basis of GQD treatment (R2X 0.505, R2Y 0.997, Q2 0.945) (Fig. S2). Volcano plots were constructed using a fold change of ≥ 2 or ≤ 0.5 and a VIP value of ≥ 1 as identification criteria to illustrate the screening results. A total of 203 metabolites (160 up-regulated and 43 down-regulated) were determined to be differential metabolites between the control and test groups (Fig. 2c). The differential metabolites were further classified into 20 biochemical groups, for example, glycerol phospholipids (GPs), sphingolipids (SLs), and glycerides (GLs). A heatmap of the hierarchical clustering analysis provided an overview of the metabolomic changes in RAW 264.7 macrophages after exposure to GQDs (Fig. 2d). KEGG pathway analysis revealed differences in the metabolic function profiles following exposure to GQDs (Fig. 2e), identifying 20 enriched metabolic pathway subcategories. On the basis of P-values, the top six affected metabolic pathways were glycerophospholipid metabolism, choline metabolism in cancer, phospholipase D signaling pathway, sphingolipid metabolism, and inositol phosphate metabolism. Thus, metabolomics indicates that exposure to GQDs disrupts lipid metabolism in macrophages. Glycerophospholipid-derived lipid mediators include phosphatidylinositol, eicosanoids, docosanoids, lysophospholipids, fatty acids, and oxidized phospholipids (Farooqui, 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metabolic flux characterization of macrophages exposed GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Metabolic flux analysis (MFA) can reveal the metabolic status of a cell, helping to systematically describe its cellular metabolic characteristics (Liang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021) and providing mechanistic insights into its pathophysiology (Yao, 2021). Metabolomics enabled the high-throughput characterization of a large number of small molecule metabolites, while MFA tracked de novo fatty acid synthesis using stable isotope labeling. Fatty acids are converted to acyl-CoAs that promote mitochondrial respiration through the generation of energy by β-oxidation and OXPHOS of fatty acids (Carracedo et al., 2013; Voss et al., 2019). Using powerful MFA, arachidonate (C20:4), docosanoic acid (C22:0), eicosatrienoic acid (C20:3), and lignoceric acid (C24:0) were shown to have significantly up-regulated isotopomer abundances relative to the control groups. The MFA heatmap shows that long-chain fatty acids accumulate after treatment with GQDs for 24 and 48 h ( Fig. 3a). In particular, eicosatrienoic acid was identified, among many arachidonic acid metabolites, as having adverse side effects associated with GQDs exposure. This is a lipid mediator derived from arachidonic acid through the cytochrome P450 epoxygenase pathway (Li et al., 2011; Szczuko et al., 2020). The cellular mitochondrial electron transport chain, cytochrome P450, and nicotinamide adenosine dinucleotide phosphate oxidase are important sources of ROS. High ROS and antioxidant enzymes (SOD1 and GPX4) place macrophage cells at risk of damage mediated by oxidative stress (Yang and Stockwell, 2016). Erucate-induced production of ROS was rapid and transient, starting immediately after exposure of the cells to erucic acid when maximum production of ROS occurred (Heiskanen and Savolainen, 1997). Increased lipid peroxidation products, decreased antioxidant capacity, and reduced glutathione (GSH) levels all promoted oxidative stress (Rani et al., 2016).

The quantitative synthesis of lipids originating from glucose-6-phosphate (G6P) was revealed by analysis of the glucose (U-13C6) flux. Labeled pyruvate was converted to M+ 2 acetyl-CoA, which provided a single precursor for fatty acid and lipid synthesis. Fatty acids can be processed via mitochondrial β-oxidation into acetyl-CoA that enters the tricarboxylic acid cycle and enhances ATP generation. MFA revealed the intracellular macrophage carbon distribution and identified key lipid metabolic processes following exposure to GQDs (Fig. 3b). The combination of MFA and metabolomics elucidated the pathways and mechanisms involved in energy production and lipid metabolism in macrophages exposed to GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Correlation analysis between proteomics and metabolomics
ce:section.ce:para: “Omics” analyses are powerful tools providing insight into the cellular processes of organisms and deeper understanding of biological responses than can be obtained from conventional analyses (Gunsolus and Haynes, 2016). Proteomics and metabolomics data were integrated to investigate the underlying basis of GQD toxicity in macrophage cells. According to the available proteomic data (Shao et al., 2021), the 82 differentially expressed proteins were investigated via enrichment analysis using Metascape to determine the KEGG pathways and GO terms enriched for the proteins of interest. Of the 42 proteins with significantly increased abundance, enrichment was evident in those involved with lysosomes, regulation of superoxide metabolic processes, mitochondrial membrane organization, and other pathways (Fig. S3a). Of the 40 proteins with significantly decreased abundance, enrichment was evident in those involved in the metabolism of lipids, glutathione metabolism, phagosomes, phospholipid metabolic processes, and other pathways (Fig. S3b). Given the antioxidant role of glutathione (Zhou et al., 2017), these results suggest that oxidative stress is a significant mechanism in the toxicity of GQDs.

Based on these results, GQDs had a significant effect on sphingolipid metabolism (ko00600), adipocytokine signaling pathway (ko04920), cAMP signaling pathway (ko04024), AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) signaling pathway (ko04152), glutathione metabolism (ko00480), OXPHOS (ko00190), and glycerolipid metabolism (ko00561) ( Fig. 4 and Table S1). The combined proteomics and metabolomics data demonstrated that most of the affected pathways in macrophages were related to lipid content, lipid oxidation, and mitochondrial function. Adipocytokines, such as adiponectin, leptin, and resistin, are secreted by adipose cells and directly impact glucose and lipid metabolism and inflammation (Tilg and Moschen, 2006). Adiponectin activated AMPK and suppressed macrophage functions and inflammatory processes. When macrophage cells were treated with GQDs, AMPK maintained cellular energy homeostasis by activating catabolic pathways involved in ATP generation, such as glycolysis, mitochondrial biogenesis, and fatty acid oxidation (Carling et al., 2011; Dermaku-Sopjani and Sopjani, 2019). The glutamate metabolism signaling pathway and glutaminolysis contributed to increasing ROS levels in the macrophages (Xu et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2017), resulting in lysosome membrane permeabilization. An imbalance between ROS production and the cellular antioxidant defense system will lead to oxidative stress and potential DNA damage.

The corheatmap revealed the correlation between proteins and metabolites categorized into various classes. The rows represent proteins and the columns represent metabolites, with red and green representing positive and negative correlations between proteins and metabolites, respectively ( Fig. 5a). Metabolite-protein canonical correspondence analysis (CCA) is illustrated for the following pathways: AMPK signaling pathway, OXPHOS, and glycerolipid metabolism (Fig. 5b-d). AMPK is an energy sensor that modulates fatty acid metabolism via phosphorylation of downstream acetyl-CoA carboxylase (Chen et al., 2018). Platelet glycoprotein 4 (Cd36) and, especially, stearoyl-CoA desaturase 4 (Scd4) were upregulated in macrophages after 48 h of GQDs treatment. The up-regulation of Scd4 expression may be one of the mechanisms by which leptin exerts its lipotoxic effects (Miyazaki et al., 2003). OXPHOS has an important role in mitochondrial respiration, an enzymatic pathway that generates ATP. Coenzyme Q10, which enters the OXPHOS pathway following oxidation, was upregulated. The main second messenger affecting mitochondrial signal transduction was cAMP. OXPHOS-promoted fatty acid oxidation should relieve the toxicity caused by accumulated lipid metabolites (Nsiah-Sefaa and McKenzie, 2016). Most of these metabolic disorders are related to the ratio of unsaturated to saturated fatty acids (Kalsbeek et al., 2016). The type and concentration of saturated fatty acids were significantly up-regulated in the glycerolipid pathway, while unsaturated fatty acids were down-regulated.

A prominent characteristic of GQDs exposure in macrophages is a disordered metabolic state, affecting, for example, glycerophospholipid metabolism and the sphingolipid signal pathway. This generated higher ATP levels and induced OXPHOS. The dynamic lipid flux distribution revealed changes in the levels of individual lipids. MFA unraveled the mechanisms of the impact of GQDs on the dynamic carbon flux distribution in macrophages, improving our understanding of the link between carbon flux and lipid metabolism. GQDs were cytotoxic to macrophages, with changes to proteins related to the AMPK and OXPHOS metabolic pathways, which were positively associated with energy and lipid metabolism. There has been a wide variety of biomedical applications using GQDs, but the extent of nanomaterial toxicity has not received much attention. The use of GQDs as a medical tool has opened new avenues of research. By elucidating the macrophage metabolic pathways impacted by exposure to GQDs, this study supports the development of nanomedical diagnostic and treatment applications. Ongoing research is required to ensure the safety and non-toxicity of such applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.tox.2022.153389.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

Supplementary material
.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Ayoubi et al., 2017
